result:
The old woman at the corner table orders the same thing every morning: black coffee, no sugar, and silence. I've been watching her for three weeks now, not in a creepy way, just in the way you notice patterns when you work the early shift at a diner that smells perpetually of bacon grease and yesterday's disappointments.

She brings a different book each day, paperbacks with cracked spines and yellowed pages that look like they've been loved half to death. Sometimes her lips move when she reads. Sometimes she laughs—a quiet sound like wind chimes on a still day. I've started making her coffee before she arrives, timing it so the steam is still rising when she settles into her seat at 5:47 AM, precise as clockwork.

This morning is different. This morning she looks at me when I set down her cup.

"You're wondering about my story," she says. It's not a question.

I freeze, coffeepot in hand, caught in the act of caring about a stranger. "I didn't mean to—"

"Sit," she says, and somehow I do, even though Marcus will definitely yell at me later for abandoning my tables. "Everyone has a before and an after. Mine just happens to be more visible than most."

She shows me her hands. There are scars there, white lines like roadmaps to somewhere I've never been. "I was a surgeon," she says. "Twenty-three years. Saved lives, lost lives, held hearts in my hands—literally. Then one day my own hands started shaking. Essential tremor, they called it. Essentially the end of everything I was."

The diner feels smaller suddenly, like the walls are leaning in to listen.

"I thought about ending it," she continues, matter-of-fact as if discussing the weather. "What's a surgeon who can't hold a scalpel? But then I realized I'd been so busy fixing other people's bodies that I'd never really lived in my own. So I started reading all the books I'd bought but never opened. Started tasting my coffee instead of just consuming it. Started existing in the present tense instead of always rushing toward the next surgery, the next save, the next proof that I mattered."

Marcus is glaring at me from behind the counter, but I don't move. Can't move.

"You make good coffee," she tells me. "Every morning, it's perfect. That's not nothing, you know. You give me thirty minutes of peace before the world wakes up and remembers to be loud. That's a kind of saving too."

I want to tell her that I'm nobody, just a twenty-something disaster taking a gap year that's turned into three, pouring coffee and avoiding my parents' calls about graduate school. But maybe that's not the point. Maybe the point is that we're all somebody's before and after, even when we don't know it.

She returns to her book, and I return to my tables, but something has shifted. The morning light coming through the greasy windows looks different, more golden, more intentional. The other regulars filter in—Tommy the construction worker who's teaching himself Spanish through a phone app, Mercedes who writes poetry on napkins and leaves them behind like breadcrumbs, the young couple who sit on the same side of the booth and share everything, even their silence.

I realize the diner is full of stories I've been too busy or too afraid to hear. We're all islands pretending to be complete, when really we're an archipelago, connected beneath the surface by things we can't see but somehow feel.

When my shift ends, I do something I've never done before. I sit at the counter and order my own coffee. Black, no sugar. Marcus raises an eyebrow but says nothing. The old woman is gone, but her book remains on the table—deliberately forgotten, I think. It's a collection of Mary Oliver poems, dog-eared and annotated in pencil. On the inside cover, there's an inscription: "For whoever needs this next."

I open to a random page and find these words underlined: "Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?"

The question hangs in the air like steam from a fresh cup of coffee. I don't have an answer yet, but for the first time in three years, I think that's okay. Maybe life isn't about the big saves or the perfect plans. Maybe it's about showing up at 5:47 AM with shaking hands and a willingness to be present. Maybe it's about making perfect coffee for imperfect strangers. Maybe it's about recognizing that we're all walking around with our befores and afters, our tremors and our scars, our poetry written on napkins.

I finish my coffee and leave the book where I found it. Someone else will need it tomorrow. But I take the question with me, tuck it into my pocket like a seed that might, given enough time and the right conditions, grow into something worth tending.

The bell above the door chimes as I leave, a small sound that means both goodbye and hello, ending and beginning. Outside, the world is waking up, remembering to be loud. But I carry the silence with me now, the kind that tastes like black coffee and sounds like wind chimes on a still day, the kind that makes room for stories to unfold, one perfect, imperfect morning at a time.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
